questions.question: Define the class P and the class NP. Provide an example of a problem in P and a problem in NP, and briefly explain why each belongs to its respective class.
keyPoints: P: problems solvable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine
NP: problems whose solutions can be verified in polynomial time by a deterministic TM
Example in P: sorting (e.g., Merge Sort) – O(n log n) time
Example in NP: Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) – given a certificate (assignment), verification is polynomial

questions.question: State the Cook–Levin theorem. Name two NP‑Complete problems and give a brief description of one of them.
keyPoints: Cook–Levin theorem: Boolean SAT is NP‑Complete (the first NP‑Complete problem)
Two NP‑Complete problems: SAT, Clique, Vertex Cover, 3‑SAT, Hamiltonian Cycle, etc.
Clique: given a graph G and integer k, does G contain a clique of size ≥ k?
Vertex Cover: given a graph G and integer k, does G have a vertex cover of size ≤ k?

questions.question: Prove that the Vertex Cover problem is NP‑Complete. You should (i) show that Vertex Cover is in NP, (ii) select a known NP‑Complete problem to reduce from, (iii) give the polynomial‑time reduction algorithm, and (iv) argue correctness.
keyPoints: Vertex Cover ∈ NP: a certificate (the set of k vertices) can be verified in polynomial time by checking each edge
Reduction from Clique: given instance (G, k) of Clique, construct complement graph G' where edge exists iff it does not in G; set k' = |V| - k
Transformation runs in O(|V|+|E|) time, hence polynomial
Proof of equivalence: G has a clique of size k ⇔ G' has a vertex cover of size |V|-k; thus Vertex Cover is NP‑Complete

questions.question: Explain the notion of polynomial‑time many‑one reduction. Use it to prove that the Hamiltonian Cycle problem is NP‑Hard. Outline a reduction from 3‑SAT (or SAT) to Hamiltonian Cycle.
keyPoints: Definition: A problem A is polynomial‑time many‑one reducible to B (A ≤_p B) if there exists a polynomial‑time computable function f mapping instances of A to instances of B such that answer to A is 'yes' iff answer to f(instance) is 'yes'
Hamiltonian Cycle (HC) is in NP: a certificate (the cycle list of vertices) can be checked in polynomial time
Reduction from 3‑SAT: construct a graph with gadget for each variable (path) and clause (selection gadget) ensuring a cycle can visit all vertices only if each clause is satisfied
Construction runs in polynomial time (linear in number of variables and clauses); a satisfying assignment yields a Hamiltonian cycle and vice‑versa, establishing NP‑Hardness of HC
